feed.title: ale627
feed.subtitle: I'm an analyst, sailor, & trader near SF. ghpages+jekyll+minima+arimo/inconsolata

entry.title: Sharks & Knights
entry.content:
I got the last minute invite to join Brian with his extra ticket at the Sharks preseason NHL game last night. His cousin Eric worked at MicroSemi, a semiconductor company which had a suite at SAP Center.

It was a gathering of Nvidia and MicroSemi folks who work together, and then us. San Jose Sharks vs the brand spankin’ new Golden Knights from Las Vegas. Apparently, instead of being composed of only free agents, what happens when a new team is chartered, one guy explained, is each team reserves a certain number of players, then the new team goes around and selects a couple unreserved players from each team to form the new team.

There was catered beer and wine, hotdogs, sliders, and cookies. The suites, which run around the whole stadium, are basically in the nosebleed section, but due to the angle of the seating and the rink lighting, you can see the puck at all times.

The game was exciting with 7 goals total. No scores in the first period, with 4 in a row by San Jose in the 2nd. At this point I admit to betting Brian $5 on the Knights scoring. Final score San Jose 5 - Las Vegas 2.
Good fun! Thanks again to Brian’s cousin Eric and MicroSemi for having us.
entry.summary: I got the last minute invite to join Brian with his extra ticket at the Sharks preseason NHL game last night. His cousin Eric worked at MicroSemi, a semiconductor company which had a suite at SAP Center.

entry.title: Camping Mendocino
entry.content:
Before this next weekend gets started, I wanted to get the details of my trip to Mendocino on paper.

Jake, Brian, and I camped at Van Damme State Park fri/sat. (Van Damme, gurl)

It was a 5hr drive from San Jose in rush hour traffic! Luckily we did take the scenic routes closer to the coast, which ended up being further, but without traffic and along 2 lane roads. As Google Maps tends to adjust ETA adding up to half an hour as traffic rises, we definitely saved time thinking through our route.

Tortellini, Smores, Quesadillas, Mandarin Oranges, Oatmeal.

The places we went to:

  • Glass Beach, to see the glass and swim. Cold water lots of kelp! The camp ranger told us Glass Beach used to be a literal dump, then the county was told to clean it up. All the broken bottles became glass beach. Now that’s good marketing. We talked to an abalone diver who was tagging some shells, and he explained he was part of a competition taking place today.

  • Caspar Beach, where a Triton Free Diving competition weigh in was going on. We saw abalone, cod, and scallops the size of a baseball mitt.

  • North Coast Brewery, home of Brother Thelonius Belgian Style Abbey Ale (appley) and the Rasputin Russian Imperial Stout (chocolatey).

  • Pygmy Forest Trail, a short hike. Pygmy apparently means short, and this forest’s stunted growth is caused by poor soil, as the area used to be underwater many years ago. There were huckleberrys (very edible!) along the boardwalk through the pygmy forest, followed by a wide fire trail to the next intersection about 2 1/2 miles up, surrounded by redwoods.

  • Big River beach, to enjoy a beer and catch the sunset after the hike. The sun sets over the point instead of the ocean, so you get to enjoy the flash of the sun setting, then a few extra minutes of light to finish your beer as the actual sunset occurs. The area in the back, a broad beach along the big river, looked excellent for hanging out during the day. (noted)

  • Russian River Brewery, home of Pliny the Elder/Younger. A great pit stop on the way back. It was very busy with locals on Sunday as it is happy hour all day, so we ate down the street at a burger joint and came back. I tried the Golden Ale and grabbed a growler of Pliny.
entry.summary: Before this next weekend gets started, I wanted to get the details of my trip to Mendocino on paper.

entry.title: Meeting Finless Foods
entry.content:
I met some cool people in a roundabout way last week.

Zois -my housemate Ian’s friend from time in Chile- stayed on our couch for a few days. He invited me to Massive Attack at 1015 Folsom with Emily, a friend visiting SF. Emily was staying in SoMa with Mike, who she met in Taiwan.

Mike lives with Brian, and together they run Finless Foods, a new food startup creating fish without fishing or farming. They’re growing fish meat from stem cells. Finless is creating surimi (fish paste) in the lab now.

  • Their fish is healthier than caught fish which contains mercury and plastic.
  • It is also healthier than farmed fish which contains antibiotics and hormones.
  • As the fish is grown without a body of water, production can be geographically distributed.
  • Since no fish are harmed, Finless appeals to ethical vegans.

An interesting question my housemate Ian asked is: can this fish be produced in a way that is less carbon intensive than the usual process?

When Zois and I picked up Mike, Brian, and co-worker Jihyun, they had just wrapped up an initial tasting. “This is three quarters of the company in the backseat, right now!” they joked as they piled in. Mike was looking forward to “only” having to speak in front of 1000 people the following week. Easy, in comparison.

Zois, Emily, Mike, and I went to Massive Attack together. It was a fun show, closing with “Teardrop”. Afterwards, we took a peek at the IndieBio incubator which was right around the corner.

In the morning, Mike mentioned that he had actually felt unstressed for the first time in months. Balance is important! (An hour later he was off to New York on a plane.)

Here’s the link to the “1000 people” talk mentioned above - IndieBio SF Demo Day 5.

Mike comes on just before the 1:03:00 mark.

Good luck to Finless Foods!!
entry.summary: I met some cool people in a roundabout way last week.

entry.title: What is this?
entry.content:
What is a blog for? You see a ton of blogs being created. Usually they only have a handful of posts from a few years ago. How the heck can this blog become anything different?

The reason so many blogs deprecate is: the owners don’t really care about them! At the end of the day, these bloggers are not actually writing for themselves. They’re writing to some perceived high-value audience and putting on a persona in order to write TO them.

Turns out, that sort of pandering is inauthentic, so it’s boring. And unsustainable on the long run. So guess what? This is going to be the exact opposite. I’m going to write randomly, and only about things that interest me. There will be no minimum length post, and no general topic.

Buckle up!
entry.summary: What is a blog for? You see a ton of blogs being created. Usually they only have a handful of posts from a few years ago. How the heck can this blog become anything different?
